summary:
The founding of the Dominican (1216 AD) and Franciscan (1209 AD) Orders — the two great mendicant ('begging') orders of medieval Catholicism — represented a fundamental innovation in the organization of the Western Church, creating mobile orders of friars committed to poverty, preaching, and intellectual engagement rather than the fixed monastic life of Benedictine and Cistercian communities. [[event:2292|St. Francis of Assisi]] founded the Franciscans with a radical commitment to poverty and joyful service to the poor; Dominic de Guzmán founded the Dominicans to combat the [[event:3060|Cathar heresy]] through learned preaching, creating what became the Church's primary intellectual order.

The mendicant orders transformed medieval religion and education. The Dominicans (Order of Preachers) staffed the [[event:2287|Inquisition]], dominated the emerging universities of Paris and Oxford, and produced the greatest theologians of the scholastic period including [[event:2293|Thomas Aquinas]] and Albertus Magnus. The Franciscans produced Roger Bacon (experimental science pioneer), Duns Scotus, and William of Ockham. Both orders established schools in cities, creating an urban intellectual presence that the rural monasteries lacked. The mendicants also led the Catholic Church's global missionary work: Franciscan and Dominican friars were the primary missionaries in China (under the Mongols), the Mongol heartland, Africa, and the Americas, following in the wake of — and sometimes preceding — Portuguese and Spanish explorers. The mendicant commitment to learning, combined with their poverty and mobility, made them the most dynamic and intellectually influential element of medieval Catholic culture.
